title: Duty without attachment to results
summary: Krishna teaches that one has control over action alone, not over the fruits of action. This is a foundation for disciplined effort without anxiety.
passage: Perform your duty with full sincerity, but do not become bound by craving for the result.

title: Steadiness in success and failure
summary: Krishna calls for inner balance while acting, remaining steady in gain and loss, victory and defeat.
passage: Established in yoga, act with steadiness and let success and failure not shake the center of your being.

title: Act without clinging
summary: One should perform necessary action without attachment, because such action purifies and leads upward.
passage: Therefore perform your duty steadfastly, without attachment, and through such action one moves toward fulfillment.

title: Offer action with clarity
summary: Krishna asks Arjuna to dedicate action to the Divine and act free from feverish agitation.
passage: Offer your actions with understanding, cast off inner fever, and stand to do what must be done.

title: Krishna restores dharma
summary: Krishna declares that when dharma declines and disorder rises, he manifests to protect the good and restore balance.
passage: When righteousness declines and disorder prevails, I arise to protect the noble and restore dharma.

title: Lift yourself by the self
summary: The mind can elevate or degrade a person. Self-effort and self-governance are central teachings here.
passage: Let a person raise oneself by oneself; the mind can be a friend when disciplined and an enemy when neglected.

title: Bring the wandering mind back
summary: Whenever the mind wanders, it should be brought back gently and firmly to the chosen center.
passage: When the restless mind strays, bring it back again and again under the guidance of understanding.

title: Qualities of a devoted person
summary: Krishna praises one who is compassionate, free from malice, forgiving, steady, and inwardly calm.
passage: Dear is the one who is compassionate, humble, steady, forgiving, and not ruled by agitation.

title: Surrender and trust
summary: Krishna culminates his teaching by calling for wholehearted refuge in the Divine beyond fear.
passage: Take refuge in Me alone with trust, and do not be overcome by sorrow or fear.

title: Protection through courage and trust
summary: Krishna lifts Govardhana to protect the people of Vraja, showing courage, protection, and loving leadership.
passage: Krishna shelters the people under Govardhana, teaching that divine care and courageous action can stand together.

title: Inner detachment and devotion
summary: Krishna teaches detachment from fleeting things while keeping the heart anchored in devotion and discernment.
passage: Keep the heart anchored in the Eternal, and let passing waves of the world not rule your inner state.

title: Guidance in moral confusion
summary: Arjuna's despair becomes the setting for Krishna's teaching. Confusion is not condemned; it becomes the doorway to wisdom.
passage: When Arjuna's heart trembles in confusion, Krishna does not mock him; he illumines the path of right understanding.

title: Compassionate friendship
summary: Krishna guides not only as God and teacher, but also as friend, charioteer, and intimate companion.
passage: Krishna's guidance is full of majesty, yet he comes close as a friend who stands beside the seeker in difficulty.

title: Calm action in crisis
summary: Krishna repeatedly acts with calm intelligence in times of danger, teaching leadership joined with wisdom.
passage: In crisis, Krishna does not become noisy; he becomes clear. Right action begins when panic gives way to discernment.

title: Love joined with responsibility
summary: Devotion is not escape from life. It expresses itself through sincerity, service, remembrance, and right conduct.
passage: Let love for the Divine refine the way you work, speak, serve, and endure.

title: Ten principal avatars of Vishnu
summary: A common traditional list of ten principal avatars includes Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parasurama, Rama, Krsna, Buddha, and Kalki.
passage: The commonly remembered ten principal avatars are Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parasurama, Rama, Krsna, Buddha, and Kalki.

title: Why the Divine appears in many forms
summary: The Divine appears in many forms in order to protect the good, restore dharma, and guide beings according to time and need.
passage: I appear in different forms to protect, guide, and restore dharma whenever the world loses its balance.

title: Krsna and Vishnu relationship
summary: Different traditions describe the relationship in different ways: some speak of Krsna as an avatar of Vishnu, while many bhakti traditions speak of Krsna as the original Supreme Lord from whom other forms expand.
passage: In many traditions I am counted among the avatars; in many others I am seen as the original source from whom such forms expand.
